task: I need to send a short note to a client about a delay and don’t want it to sound awkward. Can you draft a couple of versions for me, maybe one more polished and one more plain, so I can pick the better fit?

prefs.explanation: He values practicality and clear communication, so he likely wants answers that get to the point without extra filler. His work habits suggest he appreciates efficiency over extended discussion. He may still accept some detail when it helps avoid mistakes, but not long-winded explanations.

He keeps glossaries, checklists, and paperwork, which suggests he responds well to structured output. A clean format would fit his preference for reliability and easy reference. He probably dislikes rambling prose when a simple list or step-by-step layout would do.

His style appears grounded in clarity and avoiding ambiguity, especially when wording matters. He likely wants the assistant to say what it means plainly rather than hedge excessively. Overly soft or evasive phrasing would probably feel unhelpful to him.

His life attitude is quiet and cautious rather than emotionally expressive, so a steady tone fits best. He likely responds well to calm professionalism more than enthusiasm or hype. He may appreciate warmth, but only in a restrained, respectful way.

prefs.short: Wants enough detail to act, not exhaustive digressions
prefs.explanation: He seems to prefer information that is usable and concrete, especially when deadlines or paperwork are involved. Too much background would likely slow him down, but too little detail risks errors. He probably wants the main point plus any important caveats, not a full lecture.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here are two short options you can use, one a bit more polished and one more plain.

**Polished**
> Hi [Client Name],  
> I wanted to let you know there will be a short delay in getting this to you. I’m working through it and will send it as soon as possible. Thanks for your patience.

**Plain**
> Hi [Client Name],  
> Just a quick note to say there’s a delay on this end. I’m on it and will get it to you as soon as I can. Thanks for understanding.

If you want, I can also make them:
- a little more formal
- warmer/more apologetic
- firmer and more businesslike

per_pref.reason: Very concise and practical: it gives two short drafts with no filler, e.g. "here are two short options" and the notes themselves are brief.
Clearly organized in a checklist-like structure with labeled sections: "Polished" and "Plain," plus a short bullet list of optional tweaks.
Straightforward and unambiguous, using plain wording like "there will be a short delay" and "I’m on it" without hedging or evasiveness.
Tone is calm and matter-of-fact, not emotional or hypey; phrases like "Just a quick note" and "Thanks for understanding" are restrained and professional.
Provides enough detail to act—two usable versions plus optional adjustments—without digressing into explanation or background.
